segments.text:
cis - trans Isomerism
Disubstitued cycloalkanes containing groups on two different carbons exist as cis and trans isomers
The cis isomer contains both groups on the same side of the ring
The trans isomer contains the two groups on opposite sides of the ring
These isomers have the same connectivity but different positions of groups in space ( stereoisomers )

Energies of cis and trans Isomers
cis and trans isomers are diastereomers —stereoisomers that are not mirror images
The energies of cis and trans isomers are different
In general, the trans isomer is more stable than the cis isomer
The cis isomer contains destabilizing van der Waals interactions between the methyl groups .

Section 3.12
CONFORMATIONAL ANALYSIS OF DISUBSTITUTED CYCLOHEXANES

Disubstituted Cyclohexane Conformers
Substituents in disubstituted cyclohexanes may be axial or equatorial
To determine which chair conformer of a disubstituted cyclohexane is more stable, we must compare the numbers and sizes of equatorial substituents
For trans -1,4-dimethylcyclohexane,
The diequatorial conformer is most stable.

Disubstituted Cyclohexane Conformers-1
Substituents in disubstituted cyclohexanes may be axial or equatorial
To determine which chair conformer of a disubstituted cyclohexane is more stable, we must compare the numbers and sizes of equatorial substituents
For cis -1,4-dimethylcyclohexane,
The conformers are equal in energy.

1,4-Dimethylcyclohexanes
The diequatorial trans -1,4-dimethylcyclohexane is more stable than cis -1,4-dimethylcyclohexane, which must have one CH 3 group axial

Two Different Substituents
In disubstituted cyclohexanes with two different substituents, the more stable chair is the one with the larger group equatorial
tert -Butylcyclohexanes are essentially locked in the conformer with the –C(CH 3 ) 3 group equatorial.

Medium and Large Rings-1
Conformational analysis of medium and large rings can be complicated, but the same basic principles apply:
Minimize torsional strain. Avoid eclipsing
.interactions, which destabilize conformers.
Minimize van der Waals strain. Keep groups as far apart from one another as possible.

Classifying Polycyclic Systems
Polycyclic ring systems contain two or more rings
Polycyclic systems are classified according to the number of rings they contain
Equal to the minimum number of bond disconnections necessary to generate a completely acyclic structure
For example, adamantane is tricyclic

Classifying by Structure
We can also classify polycyclic ring systems by how many atoms rings share in common
Spiro: two rings share one carbon in common
Bridged: two rings share three or more carbons in common
Fused: two rings share two carbons in common

Heterocycles
Rings containing one or more heteroatoms are called heterocycles
Most heterocycles are named using long-established common names
Conformations can resemble those of all-carbon rings
